content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 27, 1999

Elizabeth L. Martyn
Rutan & Tucker
Post Office Box 1950
Costa Mesa, California 92628-1950

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-232

Dear Ms. Martyn:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Pro Tem Richard
Melendez regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please keep in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that
has already taken place.² In addition, this letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in
your letter requesting advice, and in the telephone conversations that I had with you on August
26, September 15, and October 6, 1999. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when
issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the
extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been
provided.

I. QUESTION

Is Mayor Pro Tem Richard Melendez disqualified from participating in a governmental
decision regarding the sale of the City of West Covina water system to a private utility company?

II. CONCLUSION

Mayor Pro Tem Richard Melendez is not disqualified from participating in a
governmental decision regarding the sale of the City of West Covina water system to a private
utility company, because even if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Mr. Melendez' personal finances, the "public generally exception" would
apply.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Pursuant to Regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

File No. A-99-232
Page 2

III. FACTS

Richard Melendez is Mayor Pro Tem for the City of West Covina (the "city").
Mr. Melendez receives water service from the city's water system. The city is considering a
proposal for selling the city's water system to a private utility company, Suburban Water. Under
this proposal, the sale is expected to result in a 10 percent or greater reduction in water service
rates for all customers of the water system. In the case of Mr. Melendez, it is estimated that a 10
percent reduction in water service rates will save him approximately $36 over a 12-month period.

IV. ANALYSIS

The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the public official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100, Regulation 18700(b).) As Mayor Pro Tem for the City
of West Covina, Mr. Melendez is a public official. (Section 82048.)

Whether Mr. Melendez has a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section
87103. Under this section, a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the
following:

•        Any business entity in which the official has an investment
worth $1,000 or more;
•        Any real property in which the official has an interest worth
$1,000 or more;
•        Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the
official within 12 months before the decision;
•        Any business entity in which the official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management; or
•        Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the
official within 12 months before the decision. (Section
87103.)

Regulation 18703.5 defines the circumstances under which a decision will have an effect
on the personal finances of "the public official or his or her immediate family" under Section
87103. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. Regulation 18703.5 provides, in
part:

"A governmental decision has a personal financial effect on a
public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses,

File No. A-99-232
Page 3

income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing."

Economic Interests

Based on the information provided to us in your letter requesting advice, Mr. Melendez
does not have any economic interests, as set forth in Section 87103, that will be affected by a
decision regarding the sale of the city's water system to a private utility company, except his
economic interest in his own personal finances, as a customer of the city's water system.¹

Once a public official's economic interests have been identified, it is necessary to
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on any of the economic interests that have been identified. There are three steps
to making this evaluation. First, it must be determined whether the official's economic interests
will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Second, the
appropriate standard must be selected for determining whether the financial impact of the
decision on any particular economic interest will be material. (Regulation 18700(b)(5).) Third,
it must be determined whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be
satisfied for any particular economic interest. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).) If it is substantially
likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the official's economic interests,
then the public official will have a conflict of interest, unless the "public generally exception"
applies. If it is not substantially likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the
official's economic interests, then the public official will not have a conflict of interest. We
stress that this is a case-by-case determination.

Direct Versus Indirect Involvement

Regulation 18704.5 states the single criterion for determining whether an official's
economic interest in his or her personal finances is directly or indirectly involved in a
governmental decision. This regulation declares that an official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances is directly involved in a decision if the decision will have any personal
financial effect on the official. In your letter, you noted that if the water system is sold to a
private utility company, then the customers of the water system, including Mr. Melendez, will
each enjoy a 10 percent or greater reduction in their water service rates. Such a reduction would
constitute a personal financial effect on Mr. Melendez, and therefore his interest in his personal
finances would be directly involved in a decision regarding the sale of the water system.

___________________________

¹ Although a decision regarding water rates can affect a public official's interests in real property, the
decision that Mr. Melendez is being asked to make is not a water rate decision, but rather, a decision about whether
to sell the city's water system to a private utility company. Accordingly, we are not including any real property
interests in our analysis.

File No. A-99-232
Page 4

The Appropriate Materiality Standard

Regulation 18705.5 prescribes the standard for assessing whether an official's economic
interest in his or her personal finances is materially affected by a decision. Regulation 18705.5
provides that "[a] reasonably foreseeable personal financial effect is material if it is at least $250
in any 12-month period." As such, Mr. Melendez' economic interest in his personal finances
would only be materially affected by a decision regarding the sale of the city's water system to a
private utility company if the decision will result in his personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities, or the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of his immediate family
members, increasing or decreasing by $250 or more in any 12-month period.

Foreseeability of a Material Financial Effect

Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision by the city to sell the city's water
system to a private utility company will affect Mr. Melendez' economic interest in his personal
finances to a material degree is the critical question in this analysis. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) Certainty is not required. Only if an effect is
just a mere possibility, is it not reasonably foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downey Development
Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; and
Smith v. Superior Court (1994) 31 Cal.App.4th 205.)

You have not given us sufficient information to determine whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision regarding the sale of the city's water system to a private utility
company will affect Mr. Melendez' personal finances to a material degree. In your letter
requesting advice, you estimated that the sale of the city's water system will result in a reduction
of Mr. Melendez' water service rates by at least 10 percent, and that a 10 percent reduction in
water service rates will amount to only a $36 reduction in Mr. Melendez' water service expenses
over a 12-month period. The information that is required for this analysis, however, is not what
the minimum reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision will be on Mr. Melendez' water service
expenses, but rather, what the maximum reasonably foreseeable effect will be.

If the maximum reasonably foreseeable reduction in Mr. Melendez' water service rates,
as a result of the sale, will amount to a reduction in his water service expenses of less than $250
over a 12-month period, then a decision regarding the sale will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his economic interest in his personal finances, and he may participate
in the decision. If, however, the maximum reasonably foreseeable reduction in Mr. Melendez'
water service rates, as a result of the sale, will amount to a reduction in his water service
expenses of $250 or more over a 12-month period, then a decision regarding the sale will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his economic interest in his personal finances,
and he may not participate in the decision, unless the reasonably foreseeable effect of the
decision on Mr. Melendez is not distinguishable from the reasonably foreseeable effect on the
public generally.

File No. A-99-232
Page 5

Public Generally

If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision regarding the sale of the water system will
effect Mr. Melendez' water service expenses by $250 or more over a 12-month period, then he
may only participate in a decision regarding the sale if the effect on his personal finances is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7)
and 18707.) For the "public generally" exception to apply to a decision, the decision must affect
the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of
the public. (Regulation 18707.)⁴ Regulation 18707(b)(1) defines the term "significant segment,"
as it relates to the economic interests of an individual, as follows:

"(A) For decisions that affect the official's economic interests
(excluding interests in a business entity which are analyzed under
subdivision (B)):
(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of
the official's agency or the district the official represents, or
(ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners, all home
owners, or all households in the jurisdiction of the official's agency
or the district the official represents.

***

(C) For decisions that affect any of the official's economic
interests, the decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are
residents of the jurisdiction."

According to the facts presented to us in your letter, the sale of the water system to a
private utility company will affect all rate paying customers of the water system in the same
manner, by reducing all of their water rates by the same percentage. Assuming this to be the
case, it appears that a decision regarding the sale of the water system will affect Mr. Melendez in
substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public, as defined above. The Act
would therefore allow him to participate in decisions regarding the sale of the water system, even
if the reasonably foreseeable effect of the sale will be a change in Mr. Melendez' water service
expenses of $250 or more over a 12-month period.

___________________________

⁴ Regulation 18707.1 specifically addresses the application of the public generally exception to
governmental decisions regarding the establishment or adjustment of "taxes, fees, charges, or rates or other similar
decisions." This regulation does not apply to Mr. Melendez' situation, however, because, once again, the decision
he is being asked to make is not a water rate decision, but rather, a decision about whether to sell the city's water
system to a private utility company. As such, the more general provisions of Regulation 18707 apply.

File No. A-99-232
Page 6

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By:   Steven Benito Russo
      Staff Counsel, Legal Division

decision regarding the sale of the City of West Covina water system to a private utility company?

CONCLUSION: •
Mayor Pro Tern Richard Melendez is not disqualified from participating in a
governmental decision regarding the sale of the City of West Covina water system to a private
utility company, because even if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Mr. Melendez’ personal fmances, the “public generally exception” would
apply.2 Pursuant to Regulation 18329, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)
